ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for a customisable grain bowl with egg and multiple toppings; FOODON_00002473 (grain-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dishes — grain-based mixed dish with egg and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers composite grain-based dishes; used as the closest defensible category for a customisable grain bowl with egg and three variable toppings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for a customisable grain bowl with egg and toppings as a food entity.

main_ingredients: whole grain or mixed grain base

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a typical grain bowl portion as served at Singaporean health-food stalls and cafes, comprising a grain base, one egg, three vegetable toppings, and a dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Grain Bowl with Egg (3 Toppings) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Grain bowl with egg (3 toppings)', CR ID F08010032, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; mixed grain and legume bowl analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database reference for mixed grain dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific customisable grain bowl. GI of 48 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed whole-grain dishes (brown rice, quinoa, and multi-grain blends) with protein and vegetable components, which consistently yield GI values in the 45–55 range. The presence of egg protein and dietary fibre (1.51 g/100g) further attenuates the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 100g calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g) / 100 = (48 × 11.03) / 100 ≈ 5.3. Glycaemic load per 350 g serving = (48 × (11.03 * 3.5)) / 100 ≈ 18.5. Reported GL per serving = 18.5 rounded to 18.5; field value set to 18.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 212 mg/100g (742 mg per 350 g serving) is borderline for a single meal component in a 1500–2000 mg/day sodium-restricted diet.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request dressing on the side to reduce sodium intake, choose low-sodium sauce options, and prefer a boiled or poached egg preparation to avoid additional saturated fat from frying.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI of 48 combined with 4.18 g/100g protein from egg and 1.51 g/100g dietary fibre attenuates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should select a whole-grain base (brown rice, quinoa, or multi-grain blend) to maintain the low GI benefit, monitor dressing sugar content, and limit portion size to one standard bowl (approximately 350 g) to keep glycaemic load within a manageable range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.3 g/100g is well below the threshold of concern; total fat at 5.84 g/100g is moderate and predominantly from egg and plant-based dressing.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose a poached or boiled egg to minimise saturated fat, select a plant-based or vinaigrette dressing over cream-based sauces, and ensure toppings include fibre-rich vegetables to support LDL cholesterol management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Egg yolk provides vitamin D and protein critical for RA patients on corticosteroids; saturated fat at approximately 1.3 g/100g is well below the RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer a poached or boiled egg, specify toppings to avoid nightshade vegetables (tomato, bell pepper) if symptoms are active, and choose anti-inflammatory vegetable toppings such as leafy greens and cruciferous vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grain Bowl with Egg (3 Toppings) is a customisable, health-oriented mixed dish popular in Singapore's contemporary café and health-food stall scene. The bowl consists of a base of whole or mixed grains — typically brown rice, quinoa, or a multi-grain blend — topped with one egg (commonly boiled, poached, or soft-cooked) and three user-selected toppings, which may include leafy greens, roasted or steamed vegetables, edamame, tofu, or other plant-based ingredients, finished with a dressing or sauce. The dish is assembled rather than cooked as a single unit, making it highly variable in exact composition. Per 100 g, it provides 113 kcal, 4.18 g protein, 5.84 g fat, 11.03 g carbohydrate, 3.1 g sugar, 1.51 g dietary fibre, and 212 mg sodium, with 21 mg calcium and 0.99 mg iron. The egg component significantly boosts protein density and provides micronutrients including vitamin D (from yolk) and choline. The grain base contributes complex carbohydrates and dietary fibre, supporting satiety and a low-to-moderate glycaemic response. The estimated glycaemic index of approximately 48 places this dish in the low-GI category, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium at 212 mg/100g is borderline and is primarily contributed by the dressing or sauce; requesting sauce on the side is recommended for sodium-sensitive individuals. The dish is classified as minimally processed, halal-compatible, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal and is positioned as a balanced, nutrient-dense option aligned with Singapore's Healthier Choice dietary guidelines. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and weight, provided whole-grain bases and low-sodium dressings are selected.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F08010032) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed whole-grain dishes, which is the approved GI source. Saturated fat is estimated from egg and typical grain bowl composition in the absence of an explicit HPB value for this sub-field.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this customisable mixed dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F08010032 as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g of 1.3 g/100g is estimated from the typical saturated fat contribution of one egg per serving and a standard grain base; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source and should be verified if a more precise value is required. 3. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed whole-grain dishes with protein and vegetable components; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. GL per 350 g serving ≈ 18.5. 4. The dish is highly customisable; actual nutrient values will vary depending on the specific grain base, toppings, and dressing selected. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical ingredients; dressing and topping selection may affect halal compliance. 6. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered.
